text.title: Scope Management Core Concepts
text.summary: Definitions of product scope, project scope, scope baseline, and scope creep — the vocabulary required to manage and defend project boundaries.

section.title: Scope Types

rule.statement: Distinguish product scope (features and functions) from project scope (work required to deliver them); confusion between the two creates phantom deliverables.
rule.rationale: A team that conflates the two may build the right product but omit required project work (testing, migration, training), or track project tasks as if they were product requirements.

section.p: Core terms every PM must apply consistently:

list.item: Product Scope — features and functions of the product being delivered.
Project Scope — all work required to deliver the product scope, including PM, testing, documentation, migration.
Scope Baseline — the approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary; the reference against which all changes are measured.
Scope Creep — uncontrolled expansion of scope without formal change-control approval; the primary cause of budget and schedule overrun.

section.title: Requirement Types
section.p: Classify every requirement at collection time to ensure completeness:

list.item: Business requirements — why the project exists (e.g., increase revenue 20%).
Stakeholder requirements — what stakeholders need (e.g., executive dashboard).
Solution / functional requirements — capabilities the system must have.
Transition requirements — what is needed to move from current to future state (data migration, training).
Non-functional requirements — performance, security, usability constraints.

rule.statement: Collect all five requirement types before closing the scope statement; missing transition requirements are the most common hidden scope.
rule.rationale: Transition work (data migration, staff training, parallel-run periods) is often scoped out by accident, then absorbed mid-project as "of course that was included."

rule.statement: Apply MoSCoW (Must/Should/Could/Won't) to all requirements at elicitation; rejecting nothing is committing to everything.
rule.rationale: Unlabeled requirements are implicitly "Must", which makes every requirement equally non-negotiable and prevents meaningful trade-off conversations when scope pressure arrives.

list.item: Must — required for the release to be viable; failure to deliver = project failure.
Should — high value but workable without; defer to next iteration if needed.
Could — nice to have; include only if time and budget allow.
Won't — explicitly excluded from this release; documented to prevent re-entry.
